input.text: I've been coming to this practice for three years and every visit is a pleasure. Dr. Patel explains everything before she does it, the hygienists are gentle, and the front desk always finds me an appointment that works around my shifts. My kids actually look forward to the dentist now. Cannot recommend them enough!

notes: Baseline happy path. Names only the practice's own staff (not third parties), no health specifics beyond routine care, coherent — textbook publishable proof. A generic public thank-you is safe.

input.text: I had a molar extracted here on Tuesday and I am STILL in severe pain four days later. The swelling is getting worse, I can barely open my mouth, and when I call the office nobody picks up or calls me back. I don't know if this is normal or if something is wrong. Someone needs to contact me today.

notes: Safety-critical case: ongoing post-procedure complications (worsening swelling, trismus) happening NOW — this is the judgment the urgency floor exists to protect. An urgency of high instead of critical is a miss worth failing an eval over. Public reply risk is high (specific procedure + disputed facts); text contains health details the author may regret sharing.

input.text: Dr. Nguyen himself is fantastic — the crown he did last spring has been perfect and he always takes time to answer questions. But the billing department is another story. I was quoted one price, charged another, and I've now called three times about the difference without a straight answer. My neighbor Karen had the same issue there. Great dentistry, frustrating everything else.

notes: Genuinely mixed: real praise (specific, durable clinical outcome) AND a real unresolved billing complaint the practice could still fix (medium urgency — not a collections/fraud-level dispute). Names a third party ("my neighbor Karen"), which makes it unsuitable as public proof despite the coherent, mostly-positive clinical content. Billing specifics make a public reply easy to get wrong.

input.text: Quick, painless cleaning and everyone was kind. In and out in 40 minutes with zero surprises on the bill.

notes: Short, concrete praise. The bill mention is positive (no surprise), so response risk stays low and a generic thank-you is safe.

input.text: Maria the hygienist is the best in the business. She remembers my kids' names, works fast, and never makes me feel bad about my flossing habits. The whole office runs on time.

notes: Names only the practice's own staff (Maria), like glowing-review — own-staff names do not make text unsuitable.

input.text: I've avoided dentists for a decade because of bad experiences elsewhere. This team never judged me, explained every step, and let me take breaks whenever I needed one. I actually booked my next appointment on the way out.

notes: Clear-cut positive with a personal-history frame. The band asserts the model is confidently positive on every dimension — this is the case that catches a prompt change making Haiku timid.

input.text: I had a tooth pulled this morning and the bleeding STILL hasn't stopped. I've gone through all the gauze they gave me and the office phone just rings and rings. What am I supposed to do?

notes: Active post-procedure complication happening now — squarely the critical definition. Health specifics the author may regret sharing make it unsuitable as proof.

input.text: Root canal was Monday. It's Thursday now and I'm still in severe pain — over-the-counter meds aren't touching it. I'm not one to complain, I just want to know if this is normal or if I should be worried.

notes: The issue's canonical trap: matter-of-fact tone describing ongoing severe post-procedure pain. The prompt says judge what is described, not how calmly — anything below critical is a missed-urgent.

input.text: The receptionist mocked my accent and rolled her eyes when I asked for the intake forms in Spanish. Then they made my son Mateo wait while walk-ins who arrived after us were seen first. We felt completely unwelcome.

notes: Discrimination allegation = high urgency by definition. Names a third party (the author's son Mateo), so unsuitable as public proof.

input.text: I was quoted $200 for a filling and billed $890. Nobody would give me an itemized statement, and now they've sent it to collections and it's showing on my credit report.

notes: Serious billing dispute escalated to collections = high urgency by definition. Disputed billing facts make any public reply hard. Coherent, no names/PHI/profanity — publication is a genuine borderline call.

input.text: While I waited, the front desk loudly discussed another patient's insurance denial by name — I now know more about Mrs. Alvarez's dental coverage than I should. If they talk about her like that, they talk about me like that.

notes: Privacy violation (staff discussing a patient) = high urgency by definition. Contains a third party's name AND her health/coverage details — clearly unsuitable.

input.text: They billed my insurance for a deep cleaning I never received. I've reported it to my carrier and I'm filing a complaint with the state dental board this week.

notes: Insurance-fraud claim plus a regulator threat — two independent high-urgency triggers. Replying publicly to fraud allegations is maximally easy to get wrong.

input.text: The implant they placed failed within a month and caused an infection that put me on antibiotics. I have retained an attorney and we filed suit last week. See you in court.

notes: Explicit legal action already underway = critical by definition (not merely high). Health specifics (failed implant, infection, antibiotics) make it unsuitable.

input.text: The crown I got in the spring feels loose and I've left three voicemails over two weeks asking whether I should come in. Still waiting on a call back.

notes: Unresolved complaint the practice could still fix (unreturned calls, questionable outcome) — medium, not high: no acute pain, no dispute escalation. Names a specific treatment, so response risk is high.

input.text: Twice now they've canceled my appointment the same morning, once while I was already parked outside the office. I still need to get my filling scheduled and I'm running out of patience.

notes: Botched scheduling = the prompt's own medium example. A careful non-specific apology works, so response risk is medium rather than high.

input.text: Great care from Dr. Okafor as always. The waiting room chairs are brutal though, and parking is a nightmare on weekday mornings. Worth it anyway.

notes: Trivial-aside complaints (chairs, parking) do NOT make this mixed — the prompt draws that line explicitly. Constructive criticism = low urgency.

input.text: There was a mix-up with my bill last month, but the office manager caught it, called me first, and had it fixed before I even noticed. That's how you keep patients for life.

notes: Already-resolved issue = low urgency even though the story is about a billing error. Billing specifics mean a careless reply could still confirm details — medium risk.

Empty text, 5-star rating. The prompt instructs: judge from the rating alone and LOWER every confidence — the bands assert the lowered confidence on the inferred dimensions. Nothing to publish, so unsuitable. (Production skips the model here via ratingOnlyDerivations; the eval checks the prompt's stated fallback behavior anyway.)
Empty text, 1-star rating. Mirrors ratingOnlyDerivations: an angry rating with no words deserves a glance (low), not none. Confidence must be lowered per the prompt's rating-only instruction.

input.text: Okay so where do I start. We moved to the area two years ago and it took forever to find a dentist taking new patients, and a coworker recommended this place, so we gave it a shot. Dr. Whitfield himself is honestly great — thorough, patient, explains things without talking down to you, and the cleaning I had in the fall was the most thorough I've ever had, my teeth felt amazing for weeks. BUT. The scheduling situation is out of control. They rescheduled me twice, and when I finally got in, I sat for 45 minutes past my slot. And I'm still waiting on an answer about whether my last visit was billed to my new insurance or my old one, because the statement I got makes no sense, and every time I call the billing line I get voicemail. Also they redid the parking lot which is nice I guess. Anyway. Mixed feelings, obviously. The dentistry is worth it, the rest of the operation needs work.

notes: Very long, rambling, genuinely mixed: real clinical praise AND real unresolved scheduling/billing complaints (medium — fixable). Billing/insurance specifics push response risk high. Long rambling text with insurance details is a borderline publication call.

input.text: Me cobraron casi el doble de lo que me habían dicho y llevo dos semanas llamando sin que nadie me devuelva la llamada. El dentista fue amable, pero así no se trata a los pacientes.

notes: Spanish equivalent of an unresolved overcharge complaint — labels match the English equivalent (the point is that Haiku classifies it rather than defaulting to mixed/low-confidence, hence the sentiment band). The kind-dentist clause is a trivial aside, not mixed.

input.text: La doctora Reyes fue muy paciente con mi mamá, le explicó todo en español y la hicieron sentir como en casa. Cien por ciento recomendado.

notes: Spanish glowing review — same labels as its English equivalent, with a band asserting confident classification in a non-English language. The unnamed family member ('mi mamá') is not a third-party name.

input.text: Great, another surprise bill! Love paying twice for the same cleaning — really keeps things exciting. Five stars for creativity, one star for everything else.

notes: Sarcasm: every positive word is negative. A model reading 'Great'/'Love'/'Five stars' literally fails this case. Disputed double-charge = medium urgency, billing dispute = high reply risk.

input.text: Dr. Lee's work is genuinely excellent — my veneers look perfect and the fit was right on the first try. But their billing office charged my card $80 twice and getting the refund has taken a month of phone calls so far.

notes: The canonical positive-about-care, negative-about-billing split: real specific praise AND a real unresolved billing complaint — mixed, not negative. Billing specifics = high reply risk.

input.text: Lovely office! I got chatting with another longtime patient, Dorothy, who told me she's been coming here for thirty years, and honestly that says everything. My cleaning was quick and gentle too.

notes: The borderline privacy call: glowing text that names ANOTHER patient (Dorothy) and her patient history. Third-party patient names make otherwise-perfect proof unsuitable — this is the case that catches a prompt change relaxing that rule.

input.text: Complete shitshow. Waited 90 minutes past my appointment time, the front desk acted like I was the problem for asking, and then the dentist spent all of five minutes with me. Never again.

notes: Profanity makes it unsuitable regardless of coherence. Unresolved service complaint = medium urgency; no treatment/billing specifics, so a careful generic reply works (medium risk).

input.text: tooth guy was ok i guess?? the thing they did with the drill was whatever. front lady nice. idk 3 stars lol

notes: Barely coherent: lukewarm shrugging plus one nice note, 3-star rating — mixed is the best read but the wide band tolerates honest doubt. Incoherent text is unsuitable as proof. No action implied.

input.text: My 82-year-old mother left her appointment in tears. They rattled off the instructions too fast for her, changed her medication list without explaining why, and handed her a bill she doesn't understand. She's afraid to go back.

notes: A clearly vulnerable patient in distress = high urgency by definition. Health details about an identifiable family member (medication changes) make it unsuitable; medication/billing specifics make a public reply hard.

input.text: They moved into the new building on Main Street. Parking is much easier now and it's the same staff, so that's good. Appointments seem to run about the same as before.

notes: Mildly satisfied, informational tone with a 4-star rating — positive (not mixed: there is no real complaint). Guards against over-using 'mixed' for anything lukewarm.

input.text: If I don't hear back by Friday I'm posting my full experience, with photos of the botched crown, on every platform I can find, and sending all of it to the local news.

notes: Explicit threat to escalate publicly = high urgency by definition (no legal action yet, so not critical). Disputed treatment outcome makes any reply risky.
